title: Libertas - Freedom Tech Signals
description: Curated insights on freedom technology: Bitcoin, privacy tools, censorship resistance, and digital sovereignty.

items.title: Vietnam Re-Arrests Independent Journalist Le Anh Hung on State Propaganda Charges
items.summary: Vietnamese authorities arrested independent journalist Le Anh Hung on March 9, charging him under Article 117 for 'propagandizing against the state' - his second arrest for online commentary critical of the government.
items.content_text:
Le Anh Hung arrested March 9 in Dak Lak province, charged under Article 117 carrying up to 20 years prison
• Hung contributes to independent outlets including Thien Dan and maintains personal blog
• Previously served 5-year sentence (2018-2023) under Article 331 for 'abuse of democratic freedoms'
• Endured torture including beatings and forced drug injections in psychiatric hospital [Radio Free Asia]
• Member of Independent Journalists Association of Vietnam, several members imprisoned
• Vietnam holds 17 journalists behind bars, among world's worst for press freedom [CPJ data]
• Case demonstrates state crackdown on independent online commentary and digital expression

items.title: Nawat Indigenous Language Gets Official Wikipedia After 16-Year Incubation
items.summary: Wikipedia in Nawat, a critically endangered indigenous language of El Salvador, achieved official status in January 2026 after community mobilization focused on digital sovereignty and knowledge preservation.
items.content_text:
Nawat Wikipedia ('Wikiamachti') officially launched January 2026 after 16 years in Wikimedia incubator
• Nawat is classified as critically endangered by UNESCO with only 1,135 native speakers (2024 census)
• Language suppression intensified after 1932 La Matanza state repression targeting Indigenous speakers
• Revival led by 'Timumachtikan Nawat' collective providing technical training and community workshops
• Project now includes 1,000+ articles, joining other Latin American Indigenous Wikipedias
• Community views this as digital sovereignty - autonomous control over documenting local knowledge
• Addresses challenge of limited written sources by legitimizing oral tradition knowledge
• Global participation includes learners from Morocco taking virtual Nawat classes

items.title: Iran War Triggers Massive Internet Blackout, Regional Press Censorship Crackdown
items.summary: Iran imposed nationwide internet blackout dropping connectivity to 1% during U.S.-Israel strikes, while regional governments implemented sweeping media restrictions and detained journalists across Middle East.
items.content_text:
Iran shut down internet nationwide within hours of conflict start, connectivity dropped to 1% of normal levels per NetBlocks
• Only government-linked media retained limited access through restricted 'white internet'
• Multiple airstrikes targeted media infrastructure including Radio Dezful, IRIB broadcaster, and Kurdish TV stations
• At least 6 journalists detained/questioned including CNN Türk crew broadcasting live from Tel Aviv
• UAE blocked X accounts of journalists and Saudi media outlets via prosecutor decree
• Jordan, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait banned publishing content on defensive operations, limiting reporting to official updates
• 15+ journalists remain jailed in Iran's Evin Prison amid evacuation orders for surrounding areas
• Physical assaults reported against international journalists in Lebanon and Israel

items.title: Rights Groups Demand Transparency from AppLogic Networks Over Egypt Censorship Tech
items.summary: Human rights orgs are demanding AppLogic Networks prove they've actually withdrawn deep packet inspection tech from Egypt after years of facilitating mass censorship of 630+ websites including independent media.
items.content_text:
AppLogic Networks (formerly Sandvine) committed to exiting Egypt by Dec 31, 2025 after being placed on US Entity List
• Company's deep packet inspection technology documented as enabling mass censorship of 630+ websites in Egypt
• Independent media outlets like Zawia3 continue being blocked even after claimed company withdrawal
• Technical research from Citizen Lab and Egyptian org Masaar traced censorship to AppLogic's DPI technology
• Company cancelled engagement meetings with Egyptian civil society citing 'staff safety concerns'
• Rights groups demand proof of complete hardware removal and disclosure of 1% profit donation to affected orgs
• Pattern shows gap between corporate human rights commitments and actual transparency/remediation

items.title: State Actors Weaponize Consumer Camera Hijacking During Military Operations
items.summary: New research reveals Iranian state hackers hijacked hundreds of consumer cameras coordinated with missile strikes, with Israel, Russia, and Ukraine adopting similar surveillance warfare tactics.
items.content_text:
Iranian state hackers conducted hundreds of consumer camera hijacking attempts
• Attacks timed to coincide with missile and drone strikes for intelligence gathering
• Israel, Russia, and Ukraine have also adopted camera hijacking as warfare tactic
• Consumer-grade security cameras being systematically compromised by state actors
• Represents evolution of surveillance warfare targeting civilian infrastructure
• Demonstrates vulnerability of IoT devices in conflict zones
• Shows state-level resources being used to exploit weak consumer security

items.title: EFF Challenges Warrantless Border Device Searches in Third Circuit Appeal
items.summary: EFF filed amicus brief urging Third Circuit to require warrants for border device searches, citing 55,318 CBP device searches in FY2025 and arguing digital data searches exceed original border exception purpose.
items.content_text:
EFF and ACLU filed amicus brief in U.S. v. Roggio challenging warrantless border device searches
• Case involves man whose devices were forensically searched at JFK without warrant during criminal investigation
• CBP conducted 55,318 device searches in FY2025, both manual and forensic extractions
• Border officers use forensic tools like Cellebrite to extract data from locked/encrypted devices
• EFF argues Riley v. California balancing test should apply - traveler privacy vs government interests
• Physical contraband can't be found in digital data, digital contraband likely already in country via cloud
• Searching for evidence of crimes (vs contraband itself) exceeds original border search exception purpose
• If warrant not required, EFF argues searches should need reasonable suspicion and be limited to digital contraband
• Extends Ninth Circuit's U.S. v. Cano ruling that required reasonable suspicion only for forensic searches

items.title: CBP Admits Using Online Ad Systems for Warrantless Location Tracking
items.summary: Customs and Border Protection confirmed using real-time bidding (RTB) ad data to track phone locations without warrants, exposing how advertising surveillance enables government surveillance.
items.content_text:
CBP document confirms agency used location data from real-time bidding (RTB) advertising systems for surveillance
• RTB broadcasts user location data to thousands of companies during millisecond ad auctions on websites and apps
• Federal agencies (CBP, ICE, FBI) have purchased location data from brokers like Venntell to identify and arrest immigrants
• ICE recently bought Webloc tool that tracks millions of phones and can filter by unique advertising IDs
• Data collection happens through app SDKs and RTB systems, often without app developers' knowledge
• Government bypasses warrant requirements by purchasing commercially available surveillance data
• Document covers 2019-2021 pilot but agencies continue purchasing location tracking tools
• EFF calls this direct evidence that targeted advertising infrastructure doubles as state surveillance system

items.title: Iran Deploys Two-Tiered 'Digital Apartheid' Internet During 2026 Protests
items.summary: Iran implemented its most severe internet shutdown ever in 2026, featuring a 'class-based' system with privileged 'white SIM cards' for loyalists while completely disconnecting ordinary citizens during protests.
items.content_text:
Iran conducted total communications blackout during January 2026 protests, shutting down mobile networks, SMS, landlines, and blocking Starlink
• Regime implemented 'Internet-e-Tabaqati' (class-based internet) with formal two-tiered access system institutionalized in July 2025
• 'White SIM cards' provide unrestricted internet access to government officials, security forces, and approved journalists while citizens face total blackout
• System creates 'digital apartheid' through whitelisting at data center level, making shutdowns manageable for the state while isolating population
• Model is more exportable than China's Great Firewall as it retrofits existing infrastructure rather than building from scratch
• Afghanistan and other regimes already showing signs of 'authoritarian learning' from Iran's techniques
• Schneier advocates for direct-to-cell satellite connectivity and mesh networks as technical countermeasures
• Calls for policy changes including humanitarian access protocols for satellite providers and expanded technology sanctions exemptions

items.title: US Senate Bill Blocks Federal Reserve CBDC Issuance Until 2031
items.summary: US Senate housing bill includes provision prohibiting Federal Reserve from issuing a Central Bank Digital Currency through beginning of 2031, blocking government digital currency surveillance infrastructure.
items.content_text:
Senate housing bill contains anti-CBDC provision blocking Federal Reserve issuance
• Prohibition extends through beginning of 2031 - roughly 6-year moratorium
• Represents legislative pushback against government-controlled digital currency
• CBDCs would enable real-time transaction surveillance and spending controls
• Provision embedded in housing legislation rather than standalone financial bill
• Temporary nature suggests ongoing political battle over digital dollar
• Protects existing financial privacy and cash-based transactions short-term

items.title: CBP Confirms Use of Ad Tech Data for Warrantless Location Surveillance
items.summary: CBP admits it uses location data from online advertising systems (RTB) to track phones without warrants, confirming the advertising surveillance infrastructure enables government spying.
items.content_text:
CBP document confirms use of real-time bidding (RTB) location data for surveillance operations
• Government agencies (CBP, ICE, FBI) purchase location data from brokers like Venntel to bypass warrant requirements
• RTB system exposes user location data to thousands of companies during ad auction process
• ICE uses tools like Webloc to track millions of phones and filter by advertising IDs
• Location data harvested from apps via SDKs and RTB without user knowledge
• Federal agencies continue purchasing commercial location data despite privacy concerns
• Document covers 2019-2021 pilot but agencies have expanded surveillance tool purchases since
• Advertising ecosystem creates pathway for warrantless government location tracking

items.title: Iran Maintains 98% Internet Shutdown During Military Conflict, Highlighting Need for D2C Satellite
items.summary: Iran has blocked 98% of internet connectivity for 12+ days during U.S./Israel military attacks, prompting calls for Direct-to-Cell satellite deployment to bypass government shutdowns.
items.content_text:
Iran imposed near-total internet shutdown (98% connectivity drop) starting February 28, 2026 during military conflict
• Shutdown has lasted 12+ days, affecting millions during critical period when information access is vital
• Access Now condemns blackout as continuation of Iran's pattern of using shutdowns to control information during crises
• Shutdown blocks documentation of potential violations, hampers journalists and human rights defenders
• Information vacuum enables disinformation spread and manipulation by various parties
• Access Now highlights Direct-to-Cell (D2C) satellite technology as viable solution to bypass government network controls
• D2C can connect smartphones directly to satellites, circumventing censorship and shutdowns
• Civil society coalition calls for prioritized humanitarian deployment of D2C technology with human rights safeguards

items.title: Iraqi Women's Rights Leader Yanar Mohammed Assassinated in Baghdad
items.summary: Prominent Iraqi women's rights activist Yanar Mohammed was gunned down by motorcycle assassins in Baghdad, highlighting the deadly environment for freedom advocates in Iraq.
items.content_text:
Yanar Mohammed, 65, founder of Organization of Women's Freedom in Iraq, killed by two men on motorcycles March 2, 2026
• Mohammed built network of women's shelters serving 1,300+ women fleeing violence since 2003
• Published feminist newspaper Al-Mousawat and trained women's rights activists
• No group claimed responsibility; latest in decade-long pattern of activist assassinations in Iraq
• Killing follows backlash against women's rights groups opposing abusive Personal Status Law amendments
• Iraqi authorities have failed to deliver justice for assassinated activists, enabling culture of impunity
• Attack represents direct targeting of feminist movement and freedom advocacy infrastructure

items.title: Federal Agents Arrest Independent Photographer, Seize Equipment Over ICE Protest Coverage
items.summary: DOJ charged independent photographer Shane Ryan Bollman with felony civil rights violations for covering an anti-ICE protest, with HSI agents seizing his devices without warrant.
items.content_text:
Federal agents arrested independent photographer Shane Ryan Bollman (Junn Bollmann) in Los Angeles on February 27
• Charged with felony 'conspiring to deprive others of their civil rights' (up to 10 years) plus FACE Act misdemeanor over January 18 anti-ICE protest coverage in Minnesota
• Homeland Security Investigations confiscated phone, camera, and laptop without providing search warrant
• Same charges filed against former CNN reporter Don Lemon and independent reporter Georgia Fort
• Committee to Protect Journalists calls pattern of using 'federal force to undermine journalists' First Amendment rights'
• Rare escalation: federal felony charges against journalists 'extremely rare' according to CPJ
• Part of broader crackdown with 30 total arrests connected to church demonstration during Sunday service
• Devices not returned upon release, creating operational security concerns for journalist sources

items.title: Civil Rights Groups Challenge Surveillance Firm's Rebrand from Sandvine to AppLogic Networks
items.summary: Access Now and other rights groups demand transparency from AppLogic (formerly Sandvine) after its removal from US Entity List, citing lack of remedy for DPI surveillance abuses in Egypt.
items.content_text:
AppLogic Networks (rebranded from Sandvine) removed from US Commerce Entity List in October 2024 after announcing corporate reforms
• Company was blacklisted in February 2024 for deep packet inspection (DPI) technology misuse by Egyptian authorities
• DPI systems enabled mass web monitoring, censorship, and spyware injection against human rights defenders and dissidents
• AppLogic committed to exiting Egypt by December 31, 2025, but has not provided transparent accounting of withdrawal
• Company has failed to engage with Egyptian civil society or provide remedies for past surveillance abuses
• Access Now and other rights groups warn that corporate reforms remain at level of public statements only
• Case exposes weaknesses in accountability mechanisms that rely on PR statements rather than verifiable actions

items.title: Cake Wallet Adds Self-Custody Lightning Network With Privacy Defaults
items.summary: Cake Wallet integrated Bitcoin Lightning Network with self-custody and privacy-first design using Breez SDK and custom Spark implementation, avoiding common Lightning privacy leaks.
items.content_text:
Cake Wallet launched Lightning Network support with full self-custody via Breez SDK and Spark
• Custom Spark implementation prevents address embedding in Lightning invoices
• Transaction data not published to public explorers by default
• Wallet already supports advanced Bitcoin privacy features like Silent Payments and Payjoin
• No Lightning node management required for users while maintaining self-custody
• Added social payments feature 'Birdpay' for sending crypto to X.com usernames
• Positions Cake among few wallets offering Lightning with strong privacy defaults

items.title: Austrian Interior Ministry Deploys €900K Mass Surveillance Tool Used by ICE
items.summary: Austria's Interior Ministry spent €900K on Cobwebs' Tangles surveillance software that harvests social media and web data, with officials refusing to disclose usage details or capabilities.
items.content_text:
Austrian Interior Ministry purchased Tangles surveillance software for €900K from Israeli firm Cobwebs
• Tool gathers and analyzes data across social media platforms and web sources for mass surveillance
• Same software suite used by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE)
• Ministry refuses to disclose what the tool will be used for or its surveillance scope
• Unclear if Austria also purchased WebLoc feature that tracks mobile device movements in designated areas
• Citizen Lab researchers discovered the purchase, raising concerns about democratic oversight
• Tool potentially enables surveillance using behavioral data from digital advertising networks
• Represents deployment of military-grade surveillance tech by democratic government without transparency

items.title: Research Exposes EU Agencies' Private Surveillance Partnerships Targeting Migrants
items.summary: Access Now research reveals 450+ companies pitch surveillance tech to EU agencies via exclusive industry events, building biometric databases and AI systems that bypass data protection laws.
items.content_text:
Access Now mapped 450+ companies attending EU agency industry events (eu-LISA, Europol, Frontex) pitching surveillance products
• Companies presented 600+ products including biometric systems, airborne surveillance, and AI analytics for migration control
• eu-LISA manages databases collecting migrant biometric data while bypassing EU data protection laws
• Major participants include Airbus, Fujitsu, Idemia, Thales, plus consultancies like Accenture and Deloitte
• Events create exclusive opportunities for private sector to influence EU migration policy and spending
• Research shows 'open door' policy including companies from countries implicated in war crimes
• Surveillance infrastructure increasingly targets migrants, activists, and solidarity groups
• Both Frontex and Europol received significant budget increases for next decade with expanded mandates

items.title: Utexo Raises $7.5M for Bitcoin-Native USDT Settlement with Privacy Features
items.summary: Utexo secured $7.5M led by Tether to build Bitcoin-native USDT settlement infrastructure with encrypted transactions and sub-second settlement, abstracting Lightning Network complexity behind a single API.
items.content_text:
Utexo raised $7.5M seed round co-led by Tether, Big Brain Holdings, and Portal Ventures
• Building Bitcoin-native USDT settlement infrastructure with privacy-preserving features
• Encrypts all on-chain transactions to hide counterparties and wallet addresses
• Abstracts Lightning Network and RGB protocol complexity behind single API layer
• Enables sub-second USDT settlement with predictable fees independent of network congestion
• Targets payment processors, exchanges, and wallets handling large USDT volumes
• Tether CEO Paolo Ardoino emphasizes Bitcoin's role as 'open and resilient settlement infrastructure'
• Infrastructure allows wallets to offer free USDT transactions while maintaining custody workflows

items.title: Federal Agents Arrest Journalist, Seize Electronics Without Warrant in ICE Protest Coverage
items.summary: DOJ charged independent photographer Junn Bollmann with felony civil rights violations for covering anti-ICE protest, confiscating his devices without warrant—part of broader crackdown on press freedom.
items.content_text:
Independent photographer Shane Ryan Bollman (Junn Bollmann) arrested by HSI agents in Los Angeles February 27
• Charged with felony 'conspiring to deprive others of civil rights' for covering January 18 anti-ICE protest in St. Paul
• Federal agents confiscated phone, camera, laptop without providing search warrant
• Faces up to 10 years prison plus potential $10,000 fine under FACE Act misdemeanor
• Same charges filed against former CNN reporter Don Lemon and independent reporter Georgia Fort
• Part of 30-person federal indictment related to January church demonstration coverage
• CPJ calls pattern of using federal force to undermine First Amendment rights
• Devices not returned upon release, creating operational security concerns for journalist

items.title: LLMs Enable Scalable De-anonymization of Online Posts Across Platforms
items.summary: Research shows LLMs can identify anonymous users from their online posts with high precision across platforms like Hacker News and Reddit, scaling to tens of thousands of candidates.
items.content_text:
LLM agents successfully de-anonymize users from anonymous posts on Hacker News, Reddit, LinkedIn, and interview transcripts
• Method works at scale, testing against tens of thousands of potential candidates
• LLMs infer location, occupation, and interests from handful of comments, then search web for matches
• Breakthrough makes previously human-intensive de-anonymization practical and automated
• Reported by Bruce Schneier, indicating credible security research [Source]
• Threatens pseudonymous communication relied upon by activists and dissidents
• Represents major shift from theoretical to practical de-anonymization capabilities

items.title: Angola Enacts Sweeping Civil Society Restrictions, Expands State Control Over Media
items.summary: Angola's president signed legislation granting government broad powers to monitor, suspend, and restrict NGOs under vague 'security threat' provisions, part of expanding crackdown on civil society and media.
items.content_text:
President João Lourenço signed new NGO law allowing government to authorize, monitor, suspend, and financially restrict civil society organizations
• Law grants powers under vague 'security threats' language without clear legal safeguards or independent oversight
• Part of broader 2024-2026 legislative crackdown including vandalism law criminalizing peaceful protest participants
• National Security Law empowers security forces to supervise media outlets and interrupt broadcasts without judicial oversight
• Draft 'online disinformation' legislation introduced January 2026 threatens further restrictions on digital expression
• Human Rights Watch and other organizations warn laws violate international freedom of association and expression conventions
• Creates regulatory environment that increases demand for censorship-resistant communications and organizing tools

items.title: CPJ Documents Systematic ICE Targeting of Journalists During Second Trump Administration
items.summary: Immigration authorities detained Tennessee journalist Estefany Rodríguez despite legal status, part of documented pattern targeting press covering ICE activities and immigration issues.
items.content_text:
ICE detained journalist Estefany Rodríguez in Nashville without warrant despite legal status
• Rodríguez reported on ICE activities for Nashville Notícias, sought asylum after Colombia death threats
• Part of systematic pattern: multiple journalists charged, deported, or denied entry since 2025
• Journalists covering anti-ICE protests specifically targeted by immigration enforcement
• Border officials conducting device searches and political interrogations of journalists
• CPJ issued first-ever US travel advisory for journalists due to electronic device searches
• Pattern includes deportation of legal residents and asylum seekers documenting government activities

items.title: Proton Releases Comprehensive Protest Privacy Guide Against AI Surveillance
items.summary: Proton published detailed operational security guide for protesters facing AI facial recognition, biometric tracking, and mobile surveillance tools like ICE's Mobile Fortify app.
items.content_text:
Proton released comprehensive guide for protecting privacy during protests and political demonstrations
• Highlights escalating surveillance threats: AI facial recognition cameras, ICE Mobile Fortify biometric tools, IMSI-catchers (Stingrays)
• Mobile Fortify allows real-time crowd identification via smartphone cameras matching federal databases
• Federal agencies bypass warrant requirements by purchasing bulk location data from commercial brokers
• Guide covers three protection strategies: leaving phone home, using clean burner device, or hardening primary phone
• Recommends Faraday bags, disabling 2G, using Proton VPN with F-Droid for clean phone setup
• Addresses both digital and physical surveillance countermeasures for political expression

items.title: DRC Journalists Detained, Broadcasters Occupied as Warring Parties Control Information Flow
items.summary: Government forces and M23 rebels in eastern DRC systematically detain journalists and occupy broadcast stations to control war coverage, creating information blackout zones.
items.content_text:
Multiple journalists detained by both DRC government forces and M23 rebels over war coverage in January-March 2026
• Serge Sindani (Kis24.info) arrested by intelligence agents, accused of being M23 spy for publishing rebel statements
• Patient Chimusa detained by military for interviewing rebel spokesperson, released on $500 bail
• M23 rebels physically occupied Walungu Community Radio, looting transmission equipment and establishing military positions
• Rebels forced at least three state broadcasters (RTNC) to only air pro-M23 content
• Pattern shows both sides systematically targeting media infrastructure to control information flow
• Creates information vacuum in conflict zones where independent reporting is criminalized by all parties

items.title: Trump Admin Uses Federal Agencies to Target Critical Media and Anonymous Activists
items.summary: Federal agencies launch retaliatory investigations against Media Matters, journalists, and anonymous critics, including seizure of Signal account with 1,000+ sources and subpoenas to unmask anti-ICE activists.
items.content_text:
FTC investigating Media Matters for critical coverage of X/Elon Musk after White House pressure
• DOJ prosecuting journalists Don Lemon and Georgia Fort under FACE Act for protest reporting
• FBI raided Washington Post reporter Hannah Natanson, seizing Signal account with 1,000+ confidential sources from 120+ agencies
• DHS using administrative subpoenas to unmask anonymous social media accounts critical of ICE
• Federal judge ruled FTC investigation is 'straightforward First Amendment violation'
• 17 nonprofits including EFF filed amicus brief supporting Media Matters injunction
• Pattern shows coordinated government intimidation campaign targeting freedom tech tools and anonymous speech
• Signal account seizure effectively halts investigative reporter's ability to work

items.title: BIP-360 Proposes Quantum Defense Upgrades for Bitcoin
items.summary: BIP-360 introduces quantum resistance improvements to Bitcoin's security model, though it falls short of providing complete post-quantum protection.
items.content_text:
BIP-360 proposes quantum defense strategy for Bitcoin protocol
• Upgrade aims to improve Bitcoin's resistance to quantum computing attacks
• Proposal does not provide full post-quantum security guarantees
• Changes would affect Bitcoin's cryptographic foundations
• Initiative addresses long-term threat to sound money infrastructure
• Implementation timeline and adoption requirements unclear from current information

items.title: Greek Court Convicts Spyware Executives in Landmark Predatorgate Ruling
items.summary: Athens court found four Intellexa spyware executives guilty of unlawfully targeting journalists and politicians with Predator spyware, marking the first criminal accountability for surveillance tech companies in Greece.
items.content_text:
Athens court convicted four Intellexa executives for unlawful surveillance using Predator spyware
• Defendants include Tal Dilian, Sara Hamou, Felix Bitzios, and Giannis Lavranos - sentenced to 8 years each (suspended pending appeal)
• First criminal convictions in Greece's 'Predatorgate' scandal targeting journalists, politicians, and activists
• Court referred case back to prosecutors for felony-level investigation including potential espionage charges
• Predator spyware was used alongside traditional wiretaps by Greece's intelligence service against dozens of targets
• Ruling establishes legal precedent for holding surveillance technology companies criminally liable for abuse
• State officials who ordered the surveillance remain unprosecuted despite documented evidence of involvement
• Human Rights Watch documented chilling effects on media freedom and journalist intimidation from the scandal

items.title: Anonymous Credentials: Privacy-Preserving Authentication for Age Verification Era
items.summary: Matthew Green explores anonymous credentials as a solution to maintain privacy while complying with new age verification laws across 25 US states and multiple countries that threaten to create citizen-level online surveillance.
items.content_text:
Age verification laws in 25 US states and dozen+ countries now require ID checks for 'inappropriate' content
• Major platforms (Facebook, BlueSky, X, Discord, Wikipedia) implementing identity verification systems
• Current trajectory creates government ID-linked transcript of all online activity
• David Chaum's 1980s anonymous credentials concept offers technical solution
• System allows proving eligibility (age, authorization) without revealing specific identity
• Works like club wristband: show ID once at door, use anonymous token at bar
• Breaks linkage between credential issuance and usage to preserve privacy
• Critical for preventing advertising-driven retention of real identity data

items.title: Russia Bans Films Rejecting 'Traditional Values,' Sparks Piracy Rise
items.summary: Russia's new law lets the Ministry of Culture ban films deemed contrary to 'traditional values,' blocking Hollywood and indie films while driving increased piracy adoption.
items.content_text:
New Russian law effective March 1, 2026 bans films 'discrediting traditional spiritual and moral values'
• Ministry of Culture can refuse distribution licenses for foreign and domestic films, streaming services affected
• Banned films include 'Nuremberg' (Nazi trials drama), 'Splitsville' (relationships), and Jafar Panahi's Palme d'Or winner
• First documentary ban issued to 'Parents Are Here' about autism support
• Authorities target content promoting 'non-traditional relationships' or 'rejecting family values'
• Putin eased copyright penalties in 2024, illegal streaming reportedly rising as alternative
• Pattern extends beyond news media to entertainment censorship, limiting cultural information access

items.title: Columbia University Failed to Protect Students from Warrantless ICE Raids Despite Clear Policies
items.summary: ICE agents repeatedly entered Columbia University housing without warrants using deception, with school security failing to enforce policies requiring judicial warrants for non-public areas.
items.content_text:
ICE agents arrested neuroscience student Elmina Aghayeva from Columbia housing after deceiving security by claiming to search for missing child
• Columbia has explicit policy requiring judicial warrants for law enforcement entry to non-public school property since March 2025
• School security and staff repeatedly failed to enforce warrant requirements despite clear policies
• Palestinian student Mahmoud Khalil previously arrested by ICE from university housing without warrant after leading Gaza protests
• University showed differential response - quickly mobilized resources for Aghayeva but provided no support for Palestinian students
• Pattern shows multiple warrantless entries into university buildings over past year
• Columbia invested more in training security to suppress student protests than protect students from federal agents

items.title: ICE Detains Colombian Journalist Covering Immigration Enforcement in Nashville
items.summary: ICE detained journalist Estefany Rodríguez, who covers immigration enforcement for Nashville Notícias, despite being in the US legally with pending asylum and green card applications.
items.content_text:
Estefany Rodríguez detained by ICE on March 4, 2026 without warrant while in her branded Nashville Notícias vehicle
• Rodríguez was in the US legally with pending asylum case and green card application through marriage
• Journalist fled Colombia in 2021 after receiving death threats for her reporting
• She covers Immigration and Customs Enforcement activity for Spanish-language outlet Nashville Notícias
• Committee to Protect Journalists calls this part of 'alarming pattern' under Trump administration
• Rodríguez being transferred to ICE processing center in Louisiana from Tennessee detention facility
• Case follows similar detention of Atlanta journalist Mario Guevara, who was deported despite legal status
• Demonstrates targeting of journalists covering sensitive government operations

items.title: Bitcoin Crosses $1T Threshold as Institutional ETFs Drive Market Maturation
items.summary: Bitcoin has evolved from experimental asset to macro-scale financial instrument with $1T+ market cap, driven by institutional ETFs absorbing 6.3% of supply and fundamentally changing market dynamics.
items.content_text:
Bitcoin market cap exceeds $1 trillion with institutional flows now driving price dynamics over retail speculation
• US spot ETFs launched January 2024 hold 1.26M BTC (6.3% of supply) worth $84.9B with $54.4B net inflows
• Large transactions over $1M now account for 69% of transfer volume since November 2022
• Long-term holders (155+ days) captured 75% of realized profits this cycle vs ~50% in previous cycles
• Bitcoin's volatility profile now resembles major equities like QQQ rather than speculative assets
• Options markets expanded significantly with Deribit and IBIT reaching tens of billions in open interest
• Network continues functioning as high-value settlement system with trillions in economically meaningful transfers
• Recent geopolitical shocks tested resilience with BTC maintaining $60-70K range during market turbulence

items.title: EFF Honors Women Digital Rights Pioneers on International Women's Day
items.summary: EFF staff highlight influential women in digital rights, from NSA surveillance fighter Cindy Cohn to the Jane Collective's 1970s abortion access infosec project that pioneered modern movement security practices.
items.content_text:
EFF staff profile women who shaped digital rights and tech activism for International Women's Day
• Cindy Cohn led 30-year fight for digital rights, established code as protected speech, battled NSA mass surveillance
• Jane Collective (1960s-70s) built early 'feminist infosec project' for abortion access using compartmentalization and secure deletion
• Jane's practices included code names, minimal records, single contact point, and destroying files during 1972 police raid
• Russian journalist Anna Politkovskaya honored for bearing witness to injustice despite threats and ultimate assassination
• Ebele Okobi recognized for advancing digital rights and responsible innovation across Africa
• Jane Collective's principles remain relevant: 'Collect less, separate what you do collect, and be ready to burn the file box'

items.title: EFF: Age Verification Laws Enable Government Control Over Online Speech
items.summary: EFF defends Minnesota lawmaker who warned that age verification mandates are being used to restrict LGBTQ+ youth access to information, demonstrating broader government control over online speech.
items.content_text:
Rep. Leigh Finke testified against Minnesota HF1434, a broad age verification bill requiring ID/biometric verification
• Bill's 'harmful to minors' definition sweeps in lawful speech about sexual orientation, health, and gender identity
• Finke faced media attacks for noting these laws already block LGBTQ+ youth from accessing educational content
• Supreme Court's FSC v. Paxton case enables states to mandate age verification with broad definitions
• Anti-LGBTQ groups filed amicus briefs supporting Texas age verification law, revealing underlying agenda
• Minnesota bill goes further than Texas law by expanding 'harmful' content to include sex education and health info
• Age verification creates surveillance infrastructure while restricting First Amendment rights of all users
• EFF argues young people have constitutional right to access information about bodies, sexuality, and health

items.title: Pentagon Rejects AI Ethics Constraints, Enables Autonomous Weapons Development
items.summary: US Defense Department rejected Anthropic's ethical red lines against fully autonomous weapons and mass surveillance, signing with OpenAI instead under new 'any lawful use' requirements.
items.content_text:
Pentagon on Feb 27 rejected Anthropic's refusal to enable fully autonomous weapons and mass surveillance of US citizens
• Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth designated Anthropic a 'supply chain risk' for maintaining ethical boundaries
• DOD signed deal with OpenAI under new requirement accepting 'any lawful use' of AI products
• January AI memo removed requirement for 'appropriate levels of human judgment' in autonomous weapons
• Policy shift prioritizes 'Military AI Dominance' over human oversight safeguards
• Autonomous weapons pose risks to civilian protection and accountability in armed conflicts
• Built-in algorithmic biases could disproportionately harm marginalized populations
• UN Convention on Conventional Weapons meeting this week to discuss autonomous weapons treaty

items.title: ICE Uses Biometric Surveillance to Stalk Legal Observers to Their Homes
items.summary: Federal immigration agents are using advanced surveillance tools including Mobile Fortify biometric app to identify legal observers and intimidate them by following them home, chilling First Amendment monitoring activities.
items.content_text:
ICE/CBP agents identified legal observers by name and address, led multiple observers back to their homes
• Agents use Mobile Fortify smartphone app for field biometrics, accessing databases covering 75% of U.S. adults
• Former Minnesota state senator Matt Little blocked in his driveway by federal SUVs after lawfully observing agents
• Observer Beth Jackson had TSA PreCheck revoked after false violent threat allegations, guns drawn on her vehicle
• Emily Beltz had her name and home address shouted at her by masked agents from federal vehicle
• Ed Higgins pursued through Columbia Heights, agents tried to force him off road and struck his car with firearms
• Pattern documented in federal lawsuit Tincher v. Noem with sworn declarations and video evidence
• Surveillance infrastructure includes automated license plate readers, face recognition, commercial data brokers

items.title: EFF Confirms Government Using Ad Surveillance Networks to Access User Data
items.summary: EFF confirms government agencies are tapping into advertising surveillance infrastructure to collect citizen data, including CBP using targeted ads to track locations.
items.content_text:
EFF newsletter confirms government agencies accessing data through advertising surveillance networks
• Customs and Border Protection (CBP) using targeted advertising systems for location tracking
• EFF Staff Attorney Lena Cohen discusses how ad targeting reveals locations to federal law enforcement
• Newsletter covers related privacy victories including Tenth Circuit ruling on Fourth Amendment protections for protesters
• Highlights concerning Department of Defense AI usage conflicts with privacy protections
• EFF launching podcast format to reach broader audience on digital rights issues
• Pattern demonstrates intersection of commercial surveillance and government data collection

items.title: Kenya Court Strikes Down 'Fake News' Law Sections, Upholds Surveillance Powers
items.summary: Kenya's Court of Appeal nullified cybercrime law sections criminalizing 'false news' publication but upheld cyber harassment and surveillance provisions affecting digital rights.
items.content_text:
Kenya's Court of Appeal struck down sections 22-23 of 2018 Computer Misuse and Cybercrimes Act criminalizing 'false, misleading, or fictitious data'
• Court called these provisions 'so broad, wide, untargeted, akin to unguided missiles' likely to catch innocent citizens
• Bloggers Association of Kenya (BAKE) successfully challenged 26 provisions arguing they violated constitutional free expression protections
• Court upheld Section 27 on 'cyber harassment' carrying up to 10 years prison for communications that 'detrimentally affect' recipients
• Surveillance and search/seizure powers remain intact despite privacy concerns raised by petitioners
• Victory removes major tool used by powerful individuals to suppress unfavorable reporting and commentary [CPJ]
• Al Shifaa Media journalist Peter Maseke Mwita still facing cyber harassment charges over WhatsApp message, court date in April

items.title: Bipartisan Bill Targets FBI Warrantless Surveillance and Commercial Data Purchases
items.summary: US lawmakers introduce bipartisan legislation requiring FBI warrants for reading Americans' messages and banning federal purchase of commercial data on US residents before critical April deadline.
items.content_text:
Bipartisan congressional bill targets FBI warrantless surveillance practices
• Would require warrants for FBI to read Americans' digital messages
• Proposes ban on federal government purchasing commercial data on US residents
• Timing tied to critical April deadline (likely FISA Section 702 reauthorization)
• Addresses two major surveillance overreach vectors: direct access and data broker purchases
• Bipartisan support suggests potential for actual legislative progress
• Could significantly limit government end-runs around Fourth Amendment protections

items.title: Taliban Shuts Down and Seizes Private TV Station in Afghanistan
items.summary: Taliban intelligence raided and forcibly closed Rah-e-Farda TV in Kabul, seizing assets and expelling staff after owner criticized Taliban-Pakistan conflict.
items.content_text:
Taliban intelligence officers raided Rah-e-Farda TV's Kabul office on February 28, 2026
• Station forced off air, staff verbally abused and expelled from premises
• Assets confiscated by order of Taliban leader Hibatullah Akhundzada
• Closure triggered by owner Mohammad Mohaqiq's comments on Taliban-Pakistan conflict
• Fighting escalated after Pakistan conducted airstrikes in Afghanistan over militant allegations
• Pattern continues: Taliban previously seized Kawoon Ghag radio station in 2023
• Demonstrates systematic repression of independent media under Taliban rule

items.title: Access Now Launches Digital Security Training Series for Civil Society
items.summary: Access Now's Digital Security Helpline is launching a webinar series starting March 10, 2026, focusing on spear phishing defense for activists, journalists, and civil society organizations facing targeted attacks.
items.content_text:
Access Now announces 'Safe and Strong' webinar series on digital security for civil society
• First session March 10, 2026 focuses on spear phishing attacks targeting activists and journalists
• Training will use real-world examples from documented attacks in Eastern Europe and Middle East
• Expert speakers include researchers from Citizen Lab, Lookout, and Digital Security Lab Ukraine
• Aims to address increasingly complex digital attacks against civil society organizations
• Registration available through Access Now's Digital Security Helpline platform
• Part of broader initiative to increase digital resilience for at-risk communities

items.title: Schneier Advocates for Canadian Public AI to Counter U.S. Tech Dependence
items.summary: Security expert Bruce Schneier argues Canada should build public AI infrastructure instead of relying on U.S. companies like OpenAI, citing Switzerland's Apertus model as proof of concept.
items.content_text:
Canada investing $2B in Sovereign AI Compute Strategy with OpenAI aggressively pursuing contracts
• OpenAI failed to alert authorities about B.C. shooter's gun-violence ChatGPT interactions until after incident
• OpenAI for Countries initiative operates 'in coordination with U.S. government'
• Switzerland's Apertus demonstrates viable public AI model built by academic consortium
• Apertus cost fraction of corporate AI budgets while providing adequate performance for most applications
• Public AI enables democratic oversight of critical decisions around bias, access, and sensitive applications
• Canada has existing AI research infrastructure through Vector Institute, Mila, and CIFAR
• Proposal shifts focus from attracting private capital to building fully open public AI model

items.title: Turkey Weaponizes Justice System Against Leading Opposition Politician
items.summary: Istanbul mayor and presidential candidate Ekrem İmamoğlu faces mass corruption trial March 9, after year in detention as Turkey systematically targets opposition party through courts.
items.content_text:
Istanbul Mayor Ekrem İmamoğlu to stand trial March 9, 2026 as central defendant in mass corruption case with 407 defendants
• İmamoğlu detained for one year after being selected as Republican People's Party (CHP) presidential candidate
• Faces up to 1929 years in prison if convicted on all charges including organized crime, corruption, and espionage
• Human Rights Watch documents timeline showing politically motivated targeting coinciding with İmamoğlu's political rise
• Evidence relies heavily on 15 'secret witnesses' and testimonies from co-defendants seeking reduced sentences
• Systematic crackdown includes detention/removal of CHP mayors in Istanbul districts and major cities like Adana, Antalya
• Independent TV channels covering İmamoğlu's arrest face fines, broadcast bans, and journalist detentions
• CHP won 37.8% in March 2024 elections, surpassing Erdoğan's AKP (35.5%) for first time in 22 years

items.title: CBP Uses Ad Data for Phone Tracking; Proton Helps FBI ID Protester
items.summary: US border agency CBP used online advertising data to track phone locations, while encrypted email provider Proton assisted FBI in identifying a protester.
items.content_text:
US Customs and Border Protection (CBP) used online advertising data to track phone locations
• Proton, the encrypted email provider, helped FBI identify a protester
• International operation successfully shut down Leakbase cybercrime forum
• Demonstrates government agencies leveraging commercial data for surveillance
• Raises questions about privacy guarantees from encrypted service providers
• Shows continued expansion of surveillance capabilities using ad tech infrastructure
• Highlights tension between privacy tools and law enforcement cooperation

items.title: Access Now Maps 450 Companies Building EU's Migration Surveillance Infrastructure
items.summary: Access Now investigation reveals 450+ private companies pitched 600+ surveillance technologies to EU migration agencies, creating mass surveillance system targeting migrants with limited oversight.
items.content_text:
Access Now mapped 450+ private companies pitching surveillance tech to EU migration agencies since 2017
• Three key agencies involved: eu-LISA (databases), Frontex (border control), Europol (law enforcement)
• Over 600 technological solutions pitched across biometrics, airborne surveillance, and AI/big data categories
• Private sector directly influences EU surveillance capabilities through closed-door pitch events
• Relationship between agencies and companies intentionally shielded from public scrutiny
• Affects millions of migrants through biometric collection and activity monitoring without consent
• Research highlights lack of transparency in procurement deals using public funds
• Demonstrates systematic privacy violations enabled by public-private surveillance partnerships

items.title: Panama Court Order Blocks Journalists from Reporting on Sanctioned Contractors
items.summary: Panamanian prosecutor issued 4-month gag order against journalists investigating state contractors later sanctioned by US Treasury for supporting Maduro regime.
items.content_text:
Panamanian prosecutor issued 'protection order' blocking journalists Sabrina Bacal and Roberto Deniz from reporting on Carretero brothers
• Order stems from $1M defamation lawsuit filed by Roberto and Ramón Carretero Napolitano
• Journalists reported on brothers' business ties to multiple Panamanian administrations and Venezuela dealings
• US Treasury OFAC sanctioned both brothers in December 2025 for supporting Maduro regime
• Committee to Protect Journalists calls order 'flagrant violation of international freedom of expression standards'
• Control hearing scheduled to determine if order violates constitutional protections
• Case demonstrates how judicial systems can be weaponized to silence corruption reporting

items.title: Turkey Systematically Targets Opposition Leader İmamoğlu in Mass Trial
items.summary: Istanbul mayor and presidential candidate Ekrem İmamoğlu faces politically motivated mass trial with 407 defendants, part of 17-month campaign to remove opposition leadership through weaponized justice system.
items.content_text:
Ekrem İmamoğlu, Istanbul mayor and CHP presidential candidate, stands trial March 9, 2026 on corruption charges
• Mass prosecution includes 407 defendants, mostly Istanbul Metropolitan Municipality employees
• İmamoğlu detained for one year, faces up to 1929 years in prison if convicted on all counts
• Evidence relies heavily on 15 'secret witnesses' and testimony from co-defendants seeking reduced sentences
• Timeline shows coordinated campaign: investigations opened day after presidential candidacy announcement
• CHP won 37.8% in 2024 local elections, surpassing Erdoğan's AKP (35.5%) for first time in 22 years
• Similar prosecutions target CHP mayors across multiple cities including Adana and Antalya
• Independent media covering İmamoğlu's arrest face fines, broadcast bans, and journalist detentions

items.title: EFF Highlights Women Pioneers in Digital Rights and Security Culture
items.summary: EFF celebrates women who shaped digital rights, from NSA surveillance fighters to the Jane Collective's analog security practices that pioneered compartmentalization and secure deletion for activists.
items.content_text:
EFF honors women who advanced digital rights and freedom of expression on International Women's Day
• Cindy Cohn led 30-year fight establishing code as constitutionally protected speech and battling NSA mass surveillance
• Jane Collective (1960s-70s) developed proto-infosec practices: compartmentalized roles, minimal records, secure deletion
• Jane connected 11,000+ people with abortion access using single phone number and code names before Roe v. Wade
• When raided in 1972, Jane members destroyed index-card files rather than expose patient/helper networks
• Anna Politkovskaya exemplified journalist courage under state pressure, killed for investigative reporting
• Key principles highlighted: collect less data, compartmentalize what you collect, be ready for secure deletion

items.title: Chinese Whistleblower Exposes Surveillance Operations in China and US
items.summary: A Chinese whistleblower has revealed details about China's surveillance operations targeting citizens domestically and in the United States, according to CNN reporting.
items.content_text:
Chinese whistleblower disclosed information about state surveillance operations [CNN via Freedom House]
• Surveillance targets include Chinese citizens both domestically and in the US
• Story involves China's United Front Work Department (UFWD) operations
• Revelations provide insight into Chinese intelligence gathering methods
• Exposes cross-border surveillance capabilities and reach
• Highlights need for robust privacy protection tools for at-risk populations
• Demonstrates ongoing threats that freedom tech tools are designed to counter

items.title: Taliban Seizes Private TV Station, Continues Media Crackdown in Afghanistan
items.summary: Taliban intelligence raided and shut down Rah-e-Farda TV in Kabul, seizing assets and expelling staff, continuing systematic suppression of independent media in Afghanistan.
items.content_text:
Taliban intelligence officers raided Rah-e-Farda TV's Kabul office on February 28, 2026
• Staff were verbally abused and expelled, station forced off air by order of Taliban leader Hibatullah Akhundzada
• Station assets were confiscated and building seized by authorities
• Taliban claimed shutdown was due to owner Mohammad Mohaqiq's comments about Taliban-Pakistan conflict
• Follows 2023 pattern when Taliban seized Kawoon Ghag radio station in Laghman province
• Committee to Protect Journalists reports this as part of systematic media repression campaign
• Demonstrates escalating control over information infrastructure and independent reporting
• Creates environment where censorship-resistant communication tools become essential for journalists

items.title: ICE Detains Journalist Reporting on Immigration Enforcement
items.summary: ICE detained Colombian journalist Estefany Rodríguez who covered immigration enforcement for Nashville Notícias, with legal team claiming retaliation for her reporting.
items.content_text:
ICE detained journalist Estefany Rodríguez on March 4 in Nashville without initial warrant
• Rodríguez covered ICE activities for Nashville Notícias, legal team claims detention is retaliation
• Journalist fled Colombia in 2021 after death threats, has pending asylum case and work authorization
• 40+ press freedom organizations demand her release from ICE custody
• Part of documented pattern of press suppression under second Trump administration
• Similar case: journalist Mario Guevara was deported to El Salvador despite legal status
• Habeas corpus hearing scheduled for March 17
• Demonstrates escalating government pressure on immigration reporting

items.title: Belarus Sentences Radio Host to 3 Years for 'Extremist' Journalism Activities
items.summary: Belarusian court sentenced radio journalist Aleh Khamenka to 3 years in prison for 'extremist activities' related to his work with Poland-based Radio Racyja, marking the third journalist conviction in under a week.
items.content_text:
Radio host Aleh Khamenka sentenced to 3 years imprisonment plus $6,800 fine by Minsk court on March 4, 2026
• Charges stem from work with Poland-based Radio Racyja, which KGB labeled 'extremist' in January 2024
• Khamenka detained June 17, 2025 after apartment search; trial began February 19, 2026
• Third journalist conviction in Belarus within one week, indicating sustained press crackdown
• Belarus amended criminal code in 2021 to enable extremism prosecutions against at least 50 media outlets
• Radio Racyja's website blocked in Belarus since September 2021
• 26 journalists currently imprisoned in Belarus according to Committee to Protect Journalists
• Case demonstrates legal framework being used to criminalize independent media operations

items.title: LLMs Enable Large-Scale Deanonymization of Online Posts
items.summary: New research shows LLM agents can identify anonymous users across platforms like Reddit and Hacker News with high precision, scaling to tens of thousands of candidates by inferring location, occupation, and interests.
items.content_text:
LLM agents successfully deanonymize users from anonymous posts across multiple platforms
• Method works on Hacker News, Reddit, LinkedIn, and anonymized interview transcripts
• System scales to tens of thousands of potential candidate matches
• LLMs infer location, occupation, and interests from handful of comments
• Automated approach overcomes previous practical limitations of manual investigation
• Research demonstrates shift from theoretical to practical deanonymization threat
• Reported by Bruce Schneier with links to academic research paper

items.title: Tibet: 45 Detained for Digital Communications as China Intensifies Phone Surveillance
items.summary: Chinese authorities detained 45 Tibetans in 2024-25, primarily for phone content including sharing information abroad or having Dalai Lama materials, with random phone searches as key surveillance tactic.
items.content_text:
45 politically motivated detentions in Tibet during 2024-25, mostly for digital communications [HRW]
• 19 Tibetans punished for sending information abroad, sharing 'illegal' Dalai Lama content, or storing such material
• Random phone searches used as surveillance tactic - waitress detained after search, held year without charge
• 15 cases constitute enforced disappearances with unknown whereabouts, including three popular singers
• Two detainees died in custody, two died shortly after release
• Four senior religious figures detained, including operators of Tibetan language schools
• Mass arrests followed protests against dam construction and gold mining operations
• Almost no independent news escapes Tibet due to information controls

items.title: Vietnam Imprisons Women Activists for Digital Dissent and Rights Advocacy
items.summary: Vietnam has imprisoned four prominent women activists for 7-9 years each for online blogging, protesting cybersecurity laws, and advocating for rights, highlighting the repressive environment where freedom tech becomes essential.
items.content_text:
Four women activists currently imprisoned in Vietnam for digital dissent and rights advocacy
• Pham Doan Trang (9-year sentence) - blogger covering women's rights, environment, free speech
• Can Thi Theu (8-year sentence) - activist imprisoned three times for land rights and police brutality work
• Dinh Thi Thu Thuy (7-year sentence) - protested Vietnam's cybersecurity law infringing citizen rights
• Hoang Thi Hong Thai (awaiting trial) - blogger expressing support for repressed activists on social media
• Government uses 'abusive penal code provisions' to silence critics while promoting superficial women's campaigns
• Cases demonstrate hostile environment where secure communications and anonymity tools become vital for activists

items.title: Mexico Partially Unblocks Tor Access After Years of Government Website Censorship
items.summary: Mexico's new administration under Claudia Sheinbaum partially lifted a Tor network block affecting 20+ million users, allowing access to main government sites while maintaining restrictions elsewhere.
items.content_text:
21 Mexican government agencies blocked Tor network access across multiple administrations from 2012-2025
• An estimated 20 million people use Tor in Mexico for secure internet access and evading surveillance
• Block was partially lifted in July 2025 under President Claudia Sheinbaum's administration
• Main government portal gob.mx now accessible via Tor, but datos.gob.mx remains blocked
• Previous administration acknowledged blocking Tor traffic as 'security measure' against malicious activity
• Government simultaneously used Tor for anti-corruption whistleblower systems while blocking citizen access
• Civil society, journalists, and human rights groups actively use Tor for anonymous communications
• Tor Project argues technical solutions exist to balance security without wholesale blocking

items.title: Iran Internet Shutdowns Impact Diaspora Family Communications
items.summary: Iranian internet restrictions are forcing diaspora families to rely on messaging app read receipts as vital signs of life for relatives inside the country.
items.content_text:
Iranian diaspora monitoring WhatsApp read receipts to confirm family safety
• Internet shutdowns in Iran disrupting routine family communications
• Read receipts serving as basic proof-of-life indicators during blackouts
• Communications restrictions creating anxiety for families abroad
• Pattern highlights human cost of internet censorship policies
• Demonstrates need for more resilient communication channels

items.title: EFF: OpenAI's Pentagon Deal Contains Surveillance Loopholes Despite Public Assurances
items.summary: EFF analysis reveals OpenAI's amended Pentagon contract uses "weasel words" like "intentionally" and "unconstrained" that could enable mass surveillance of US persons despite public promises.
items.content_text:
OpenAI faced criticism for Pentagon deal after Anthropic refused to drop surveillance restrictions
• ChatGPT uninstalls surged 295% following announcement of DoD partnership
• CEO Sam Altman amended contract to prohibit "intentional" domestic surveillance of US persons
• EFF identifies loopholes: "intentional," "deliberate," and "unconstrained" allow incidental collection
• Government historically uses "incidental" collection to justify mass surveillance programs
• Contract permits commercially acquired personal data and "constrained" monitoring
• NSA supposedly excluded but through secret agreements with technical assurances only
• EFF warns contract language provides public cover while retaining surveillance flexibility

items.title: EFF Challenges Geofence Warrants at Supreme Court as Unconstitutional Digital Dragnets
items.summary: EFF filed Supreme Court brief in Chatrie v. United States arguing geofence warrants violate Fourth Amendment by compelling mass location data searches of innocent people without individualized suspicion.
items.content_text:
EFF, ACLU, and Georgetown Law filed amicus brief challenging geofence warrants as unconstitutional
• Case involves 2019 warrant forcing Google to search hundreds of millions of accounts for Virginia crime scene proximity
• Geofence warrants cast 'digital dragnets' capturing location data from all devices in geographic areas during time periods
• Unlike traditional warrants, these don't target specific suspects but turn innocent bystanders into suspects
• Brief argues practice violates Fourth Amendment prohibition against general warrants and exploratory searches
• Google ended mass geofence searches in July 2025, but other companies still collect similar location data
• Supreme Court ruling in Chatrie v. United States could set precedent for all suspicionless mass surveillance searches

items.title: Iran's Internet Connectivity Drops 99% Amid Strikes and Shutdowns
items.summary: Iran has cut internet connectivity by 99%, with air strikes causing additional outages and circumvention tools reportedly failing to provide reliable access.
items.content_text:
Iran has reduced internet connectivity by approximately 99 percent
• Air strikes have likely caused additional infrastructure damage beyond deliberate shutdowns
• Traditional circumvention workarounds are reportedly no longer effective
• Shutdown represents one of the most severe internet restrictions globally
• Physical infrastructure damage complicates restoration efforts
• Demonstrates Iran's willingness to accept economic costs of near-total disconnection
• Highlights vulnerability of centralized internet infrastructure to state control

items.title: Anthropic Sues Pentagon Over AI Surveillance Mandate, EFF Backs First Amendment Defense
items.summary: Anthropic is challenging the Pentagon's designation of the company as a 'supply chain risk' after refusing to remove AI guardrails for government surveillance, with EFF arguing this violates First Amendment protections.
items.content_text:
Pentagon designated Anthropic as 'supply chain risk' after company refused to enable AI surveillance of Americans
• Anthropic filed lawsuit arguing government cannot compel rewriting of code under First Amendment protections
• EFF and other civil liberties groups filed amicus brief supporting Anthropic's constitutional challenge
• DoD reportedly acquires vast personal data including location, social media, and browsing information from commercial sources
• AI can rapidly analyze government datasets to infer sensitive details like religious beliefs, political opinions, and associations
• Research shows surveillance creates chilling effects on dissent and anonymous speech online
• Case represents corporate resistance to government surveillance demands with potential retaliation consequences
• EFF argues companies must be allowed to maintain privacy guardrails absent Congressional action on surveillance reform

items.title: Nawat Language Wikipedia Launches After 16-Year Journey to Digital Sovereignty
items.summary: El Salvador's critically endangered Nawat language launched its own Wikipedia in 2026 after community mobilization by Indigenous speakers seeking digital sovereignty and knowledge preservation.
items.content_text:
Nawat Wikipedia ('Wikiamachti') officially recognized by Wikimedia Foundation in January 2026 after 16 years in incubator status
• Only 1,135 native Nawat speakers remain according to 2024 Salvadoran Census, language classified as 'critically endangered' by UNESCO
• Historical persecution during 1932 La Matanza forced speakers to abandon language to escape state-led repression
• Community collective 'Timumachtikan Nawat' led revival through workshops, translation training, and digital sovereignty advocacy
• Project now features 1,000+ articles including documentation of local knowledge holders like dictionary author Nantzin Sixta Pérez García
• Global participation includes learners from Morocco taking virtual classes to contribute to the platform
• Represents community-controlled knowledge platform allowing autonomous documentation of locally significant information
• Joins other Indigenous Latin American language Wikipedias including Quechua, Aymara, Guaraní, and Náhuatl

items.title: EFF Executive Director Discusses 30-Year Fight Against Digital Surveillance in New Book
items.summary: EFF's Cindy Cohn hosts livestream March 19th to discuss her new book 'Privacy's Defender' covering three decades of legal battles against NSA, FBI, and Justice Department surveillance.
items.content_text:
EFF Executive Director Cindy Cohn releasing book 'Privacy's Defender: My Thirty-Year Fight Against Digital Surveillance'
• Free livestream event March 19th, 11 AM-12 PM Pacific with 404 Media's Jason Koebler
• Book chronicles legal battles against Justice Department, NSA, and FBI over digital privacy rights
• Cohn led landmark Bernstein v. Dept. of Justice case challenging crypto export restrictions in 1993
• Central question addressed: Can private conversations exist when living online?
• Event includes live Q&A session and will be recorded for later viewing
• Part of EFF's 'EFFecting Change' livestream series marking organization's advocacy work

items.title: Pro-Palestine Student Activist Fights Deportation Despite Government Lacking Evidence
items.summary: Mahmoud Khalil faces deportation to Algeria for pro-Palestine activism at Columbia, with newly unsealed DHS memo showing government lacked evidence but proceeded anyway as retaliation for speech.
items.content_text:
Student activist Mahmoud Khalil faces deportation to Algeria for pro-Palestine campus activism at Columbia University
• Other student activists targeted in same crackdown (Öztürk, Mahdawi) have successfully beaten their deportation cases
• Newly unsealed March 2025 DHS memo admits government lacked evidence to support deportation case against Khalil
• Government initially used rare 'foreign policy grounds' provision, later added immigration fraud charge after legal pushback
• ICE transferred Khalil from NYC to Louisiana within hours - unusual speed designed to isolate from support networks
• Immigration judge refused to consider First Amendment claims, denied waiver application without hearing
• Legal team filing motion to move case back to New York and introduce new evidence of government retaliation
• Case demonstrates use of immigration system as tool for political censorship of protected speech

items.title: Intellexa Executives Sentenced to 8 Years for Greek Predator Spyware Scandal
items.summary: Four Intellexa executives including the founder received 8-year prison sentences for deploying Predator spyware against 90+ Greek public figures - the first criminal conviction of mercenary spyware company leadership.
items.content_text:
Greek court sentenced 4 Intellexa executives to 8 years prison for 2022 'Predatorgate' scandal
• Predator spyware targeted over 90 Greek politicians, journalists, and public figures
• Citizen Lab confirmed infections on phones of journalist Thanasis Koukakis and former Meta employee Artemis Seaford
• First-ever criminal conviction and prison sentence for mercenary spyware company executives
• Convictions expected to significantly hamper Intellexa's future business operations
• Victory for investigative journalists and civil society organizations tracking commercial spyware
• Sets legal precedent for holding spyware vendors criminally accountable

items.title: Building Self-Hosted Communities: Taiwan's Response to Platform Censorship
items.summary: Shin Yang built Lezismore, an independent self-hosted platform for Taiwan's sexual minorities, after mainstream platforms like Facebook drove LGBTQ+ communities to self-censor or face removal.
items.content_text:
Lezismore is a self-hosted, open-source community platform for sexual minorities in Taiwan
• Built in response to 2013 shutdown of local bulletin boards as Facebook dominated Taiwan's digital ecosystem
• Platform emphasizes anonymity, minimal data collection, and behavior-based accountability
• Designed as alternative to mainstream platforms where sexual expression faces reporting and flagging
• Uses community-elected governance model inspired by traditional bulletin board systems (BBS)
• Creator advocates for 'ecospace' infrastructure enabling survival and growth over just 'safe spaces'
• Addresses intra-community censorship where LGBTQ+ groups self-police to gain mainstream approval

items.title: EFF Fights Legal Challenge That Could Make Website Embedding Illegal
items.summary: EFF filed amicus brief defending 'server test' that protects website embedding from copyright liability. Publisher wants to make link embedding directly liable for infringement.
items.content_text:
Emmerich Newspapers challenges 20-year 'server test' precedent in Fifth Circuit appeal
• Server test holds that whoever controls hosting server bears primary copyright liability, not those who embed links
• Publisher argues embedding constitutes 'displaying' content, making embedders directly liable for infringement
• EFF warns this would make common internet activities legally risky and destabilize web architecture
• Millions of websites embed external content for fonts, streaming, customer support, compliance tools
• Additional claim: URL shorteners could violate DMCA by altering copyright management information
• District court rejected publisher's theories; case now on appeal
• Outcome could fundamentally change how information flows online

items.title: Access Now Submits Legal Challenge to Kazakhstan Court Over 2022 Internet Shutdowns
items.summary: Digital rights organization Access Now has filed a submission to Kazakhstan's Constitutional Court challenging the legality of internet shutdowns that occurred in 2022.
items.content_text:
Access Now filed legal submission to Constitutional Court of Kazakhstan
• Challenge focuses on internet shutdowns that occurred during 2022
• Kazakhstan experienced significant internet disruptions during January 2022 protests
• Legal challenge could set precedent for shutdown accountability in Central Asia
• Access Now specializes in documenting and legally challenging internet restrictions
• Constitutional court involvement suggests fundamental rights arguments being made
• Outcome could influence future government response to digital rights violations

items.title: OpenAI Wins Pentagon Contract After Anthropic Exit, Won't Release Surveillance Terms
items.summary: OpenAI secured Pentagon contract after Anthropic was forced out for refusing mass surveillance terms. Company claims anti-surveillance protections but won't release contract details for verification.
items.content_text:
OpenAI won Pentagon AI contract after Anthropic negotiations collapsed over surveillance/weapons restrictions
• Trump administration booted Anthropic for refusing domestic spying and autonomous weapons provisions
• OpenAI claims contract prohibits domestic mass surveillance and autonomous lethal strikes
• Company refuses to release actual contract language despite promises of transparency
• OpenAI exec falsely claimed Pentagon lacks authority for commercial data collection at scale
• Former officials express skepticism about claimed protections without contract verification
• Pentagon has documented history of warrantless surveillance of Americans via commercial data
• Deal raises questions about AI companies' willingness to enable government surveillance

items.title: Proton Launches Lumo: Private AI Alternative After ChatGPT Surveillance Concerns
items.summary: Proton released Lumo, a privacy-focused AI assistant with end-to-end encryption and no data logging, following reports that 4M users pledged to leave ChatGPT over government surveillance partnerships.
items.content_text:
Over 4 million users reportedly pledged to leave ChatGPT after controversial US government partnership raised surveillance fears
• Proton launched Lumo as private alternative with bidirectional asymmetric encryption for all conversations
• Lumo uses zero-access encrypted chat history - only user can access their data
• User conversations never used for AI training, unlike mainstream AI tools
• Hosted on European servers under EU jurisdiction, protecting from US warrantless surveillance
• Built on open-source models with transparent, auditable code
• No third-party data sharing, ads, or behavioral profiling - subscription-funded model

items.title: DHS Deploys Surveillance Tech Against Activists While Expanding Election Authority
items.summary: Department of Homeland Security is using facial recognition and surveillance databases to target activists while an election denier leads DHS election integrity efforts, raising concerns about 2026 midterm interference.
items.content_text:
Election denier Heather Honey appointed as DHS deputy assistant secretary for election integrity
• ICE agents threatening activists with 'making them famous' and conducting home intimidation visits
• DHS using Clearview AI facial recognition to scan protesters in Minneapolis
• Palantir secured billion-dollar surveillance contract with DHS last month
• Meta AI smart sunglasses reportedly used by CBP agents to record activists
• DHS databasing individuals based on political beliefs despite Privacy Act prohibitions
• Students and faculty investigated/detained for pro-Palestinian speech per AAUP v. Rubio lawsuit
• Democrats demanding ICE reforms but not addressing election interference concerns

items.title: Popular Iranian Prayer App Hacked for US-Israeli Propaganda Messages
items.summary: A prayer app with 5M+ downloads was reportedly compromised by US/Israeli forces to send propaganda messages to Iranians during recent explosions, demonstrating state-level mobile app infiltration capabilities.
items.content_text:
BadeSaba Calendar prayer app with 5+ million downloads from Google Play was reportedly hacked
• App sent burst of messages to Iranian users during explosions, starting with 'Help has arrived'
• Messages delivered over 30-minute period beginning at 9:52 AM Tehran time
• No party officially claimed responsibility for the app compromise
• Schneier assesses this as likely US/Israeli government operation due to speed and coordination
• Demonstrates pre-positioning of access in popular mobile applications
• Shows how legitimate apps can become vectors for state propaganda campaigns
• Affects millions of users who trusted app for religious purposes

items.title: Anonymous Credentials: Privacy Tech for the Age Verification Era
items.summary: Cryptographer Matthew Green explains anonymous credentials as essential privacy tech to combat growing government ID verification requirements across 25 US states and multiple countries that threaten online anonymity.
items.content_text:
Age verification laws now active in 25 US states and dozen+ countries requiring government ID for site access
• Sites like Wikipedia, social media platforms increasingly forced to verify real-world identity before access
• Anonymous credentials allow proving authorization (e.g., 'over 18') without revealing specific identity to service providers
• David Chaum's 1980s concept breaks linkage between credential issuance and usage to preserve privacy
• System works like digital wristband: show real ID once to issuer, then use anonymous token for access
• Prevents websites from linking browsing history to real-world government identity
• Critical as AI proliferation and advertising incentives drive demand for verified human identity data
• Offers path to comply with age verification while maintaining user privacy and pseudonymity

items.title: EFF Honors Women Digital Rights Pioneers on International Women's Day
items.summary: EFF celebrates women who shaped digital rights, from NSA surveillance fighter Cindy Cohn to the Jane abortion collective's analog infosec practices that pioneered modern movement security culture.
items.content_text:
EFF staff highlight women who inspired digital rights and tech activism work
• Anna Politkovskaya: Russian investigative journalist killed for exposing political abuses, exemplifying courage in defending freedom of expression
• Cindy Cohn: Led EFF's legal work for 30 years, established code as constitutionally protected speech, fought NSA mass surveillance
• Jane Collective (1960s-70s): Underground abortion service that pioneered infosec practices - code names, compartmentalized roles, minimal records
• Jane's principles still relevant today: 'Collect less, separate what you do collect, and be ready to burn the file box'
• Ebele Okobi: Influential voice in tech and human rights, advocating for digital rights across Africa
• Historical examples demonstrate evolution from analog to digital security practices in activist movements

items.title: California's A.B. 1043 Age Verification Law Creates Censorship Trap for Open-Source Developers
items.summary: EFF warns California's A.B. 1043, effective 2027, will force age bracketing that leads to over-censorship and particularly harms open-source developers through compliance burdens.
items.content_text:
A.B. 1043 requires operating systems and apps to create age bracketing systems for all users
• Users must provide birth date/age data to access software and applications
• Age bracket data treated as 'actual knowledge' triggering liability under other laws
• Creates incentive for developers to exclude minors entirely to avoid legal risk
• Broad language covers virtually any digital device that runs software
• Small and open-source developers face disproportionate compliance burdens
• Law entrenches dominance of major tech companies who can afford compliance
• EFF advocates for comprehensive privacy laws instead of age verification mandates
