items.title: EU Mandates Crypto Platform Reporting Starting 2026
items.summary: EU implementing new crypto tax rules requiring platforms to report user data and transactions to authorities, significantly reducing financial privacy for digital assets starting 2026.
items.content_text:
EU introducing mandatory crypto transaction reporting requirements for platforms
• New rules take effect in 2026, targeting tax transparency for digital assets
• Platforms will be required to report user data and transaction details to authorities
• Represents major shift toward surveillance-based approach to crypto taxation
• Affects all cryptocurrency users operating through regulated platforms in EU
• Part of broader global trend toward crypto transaction monitoring
• Could influence platform compliance costs and user privacy practices

items.title: Journalists Use Satellite Links, Encrypted Apps to Report from Internet-Cut Iran
items.summary: After Iran cut internet access following strikes on officials, journalists are using satellite connections and encrypted messaging to circumvent state censorship and continue reporting.
items.content_text:
Iran severed internet access after strikes killed senior Iranian officials
• Journalists inside Iran are using satellite links to maintain connectivity
• Encrypted messaging apps being deployed to bypass communications blocks
• Smuggled footage serving as additional workaround for information flow
• Demonstrates real-world effectiveness of censorship-resistant tools during crisis
• Pattern shows satellite connectivity as critical backup during internet shutdowns
• Highlights importance of encrypted communications under authoritarian response

items.title: Citizen Lab: States Using Zero-Click Spyware for Gendered Digital Repression
items.summary: Citizen Lab research reveals how authoritarian states are using zero-click spyware and data brokers to target women activists across borders with gender-specific harassment tactics.
items.content_text:
Citizen Lab published report on gendered dimensions of digital transnational repression in late 2024
• States use zero-click spyware and data broker information to target women human rights defenders abroad
• Attacks exploit patriarchal norms, using threats about women's bodies/sexuality to silence activists
• Digital repression causes psychological distress, leading to self-censorship and withdrawal from public life
• Autocrats can target women activists 'without deploying a lot of resources' through commercial spyware
• Researcher Noura Aljizawi, herself a victim, emphasizes community support as key to building resilience
• Report calls for better support systems for victims of gendered digital repression

items.title: Lao Government Critic Found Dead After Arrest, Highlighting Digital Activist Risks
items.summary: Sisay Luangmonda, a prominent social media critic of Laos government, was found dead after being arrested by soldiers, part of systematic repression of digital dissent in authoritarian regimes.
items.content_text:
Sisay Luangmonda (Bao Mor Khaen), 32, found dead February 20, 2026, four days after family reported him missing
• Witnesses reported soldiers arrested him outside Vientiane on February 14 and took him to Phonthan Prison
• Sisay used social media to criticize corruption and mismanagement by ruling Lao People's Revolutionary Party
• He had received death threats related to his online commentary before his death
• Authorities provided no information to family about his whereabouts and have not started investigation
• Part of pattern: multiple Lao activists disappeared or killed since 2012, including exiles in Thailand
• Demonstrates real-world consequences activists face for digital political expression
• No attacks on Lao dissidents have been resolved or prosecuted to date

items.title: EFF Challenges Geofence Warrants at Supreme Court as Unconstitutional Digital Dragnets
items.summary: EFF and civil liberties groups filed Supreme Court brief arguing geofence warrants violate Fourth Amendment by turning location data into mass surveillance dragnets that make suspects of innocent bystanders.
items.content_text:
EFF, ACLU, and Georgetown Law filed amicus brief in Chatrie v. United States case
• Geofence warrants compel companies to provide data on every device in geographic area during time period
• 2019 Google warrant covered area size of several football fields, searched hundreds of millions of accounts
• Brief argues these are 'general warrants' that Fourth Amendment specifically prohibits
• Unlike traditional warrants, no specific suspect or device targeted - creates digital dragnet
• Google ended mass geofence searches in July 2025, but other companies still collect location data
• Supreme Court ruling could establish precedent protecting against suspicionless location surveillance

items.title: Iran War Triggers Massive Internet Blackout, Systematic Media Suppression Across Middle East
items.summary: Iran imposed near-total internet blackout (down to 1% connectivity) as war began, while multiple states targeted journalists, bombed media facilities, and restricted press coverage across the region.
items.content_text:
Iran shut down nationwide internet to 1% of normal levels after U.S.-Israel strikes killed Supreme Leader Khamenei
• Committee to Protect Journalists documented 2 killed, 8 media outlets bombed, 7 journalists detained in first week
• Only government-linked media retained limited access through Iran's restricted 'white internet'
• Israeli forces targeted multiple media facilities including Iranian state broadcaster IRIB and Lebanese outlets
• Multiple countries imposed reporting restrictions: Jordan, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait banned coverage of military operations
• UAE blocked press X accounts; Qatar banned photographing incident sites
• Israeli authorities confiscated equipment, accessed journalists' phones without consent during detentions
• At least 15 journalists remain imprisoned in Iran's Evin Prison amid IDF evacuation orders for the area

items.title: EFF Executive Director Cindy Cohn Releases Memoir on 30-Year Digital Privacy Fight
items.summary: EFF Executive Director Cindy Cohn publishes memoir documenting three decades of digital privacy battles including Crypto Wars, NSA surveillance, and FBI gag orders as she prepares to step down.
items.content_text:
Cindy Cohn releasing memoir 'Privacy's Defender: My Thirty-Year Fight Against Digital Surveillance' via MIT Press
• Book covers Crypto Wars of 1990s, NSA dragnet surveillance battles, and fights against FBI gag orders
• Cohn stepping down as EFF Executive Director after 25 years with the organization
• National book tour launching with San Francisco and Berkeley events in March
• Book features endorsements from Edward Snowden, Lawrence Lessig, and Senator Ron Wyden
• Proceeds from book sales benefit EFF
• Documents legal battles representing technology users, innovators, whistleblowers, and researchers

items.title: OpenAI Claims Pentagon Contract Bars Mass Surveillance, Refuses to Release Proof
items.summary: OpenAI secured a Pentagon AI contract after rival Anthropic was booted for refusing surveillance terms, but won't release contract details to verify claims about domestic spying prohibitions.
items.content_text:
OpenAI announced Pentagon contract after Anthropic talks collapsed over surveillance/weapons restrictions
• Anthropic was ordered to phase out government tools within 6 months for refusing mass surveillance terms
• OpenAI claims contract prohibits domestic mass surveillance and autonomous weapons use
• Company refuses to release actual contract language despite public scrutiny
• CEO Sam Altman says deal includes stricter protections and bars NSA collaboration
• Former Pentagon officials express skepticism about claimed restrictions
• OpenAI spokesperson made false claims about Pentagon's legal authority for data collection
• Contract language reportedly includes vague caveats around 'intentional' surveillance

items.title: EFF Fights Copyright Abuse to Protect Anonymous Religious Dissent
items.summary: Watch Tower is using DMCA subpoenas to unmask anonymous Jehovah's Witnesses who created research tools exposing church document changes and failed prophecies.
items.content_text:
Watch Tower Bible and Tract Society issued DMCA subpoenas to Google and Cloudflare targeting anonymous researcher 'J. Doe'
• Doe created JWS Library website with research tools analyzing Jehovah's Witnesses historical documents and policy changes
• Research revealed failed prophecies, erasure of leadership scandals, and increased calls for obedience and donations
• Jehovah's Witnesses punish dissent through 'disfellowshipping' - cutting members off from family and community
• EFF argues Doe's research constitutes fair use and First Amendment-protected anonymous speech
• Case demonstrates copyright law being weaponized to silence religious criticism and unmask anonymous speakers
• Southern District of New York courts will decide whether to quash the subpoenas

items.title: Iranian Prayer App Hacked to Send Surrender Messages During Israeli Strikes
items.summary: Iranians received push notifications promising amnesty and saying 'help is on the way' through a compromised prayer app during Israeli airstrikes on Tehran.
items.content_text:
Iranian users received mysterious push notifications during Israeli airstrikes on Tehran
• Messages promised amnesty and stated 'help is on the way' if recipients surrendered
• Notifications delivered through a compromised prayer app
• Demonstrates weaponization of civilian digital infrastructure during conflict
• Highlights vulnerability of centralized apps to hostile takeover
• Shows how push notification systems can be used for psychological operations
• Exposes lack of user control over notifications on their own devices

items.title: Iran Internet Blackout Isolates 15 Jailed Journalists During Regional Conflict
items.summary: Iran imposed near-total internet blackout during regional escalation, cutting off at least 15 imprisoned journalists from outside contact, with three held at Evin prison in Israeli-ordered evacuation zone.
items.content_text:
Iran implemented near-total internet blackout and severe communication disruptions during ongoing regional conflict
• At least 15 journalists remain imprisoned in Iran, completely isolated from families and outside world
• Three journalists (Reza Valizadeh, Shinnosuke Kawashima, Mohammad Zare-Foumani) held at Evin prison in Tehran evacuation zone
• Evin Prison previously struck in Israeli attacks, killing 80+ people including prisoners
• Prison authorities reportedly abandoned facility, leaving special counter-terrorism forces in control
• Food shortages and deteriorating conditions reported in prison wards
• Internet blackouts being weaponized to suppress information flow and isolate detainees during conflict

items.title: Netherlands Considers Jailbreaking F-35 Jets to Break US Software Dependency
items.summary: Dutch Defense Secretary said he could jailbreak F-35 fighter jets to accept third-party software, highlighting growing concerns about US technological dependencies.
items.content_text:
Dutch Defense Secretary suggested jailbreaking F-35 fighter jets to run third-party software
• Countries worldwide expressing concern about dependencies on US technology
• F-35 purchasers currently dependent on US for all software maintenance
• Represents state-level application of censorship resistance principles
• Demonstrates how technological sovereignty affects even military hardware
• Pattern of nations seeking to break free from US platform control
• Shows jailbreaking concepts scaling beyond consumer devices to critical infrastructure

items.title: Bitcoin Core IBD Optimization Critical for Maintaining Decentralized Node Access
items.summary: Bitcoin Core developers continuously optimize Initial Block Download to prevent cheap consumer hardware from being priced out of running full nodes as blockchain grows.
items.content_text:
Initial Block Download (IBD) requires new nodes to download and verify 17 years of Bitcoin transaction history
• Without constant optimization, consumer hardware like Raspberry Pis might lose ability to join network
• IBD process involves peer discovery, header download, block download, and validation - much runs in parallel
• Key historical improvements include Ultraprune UTXO set (2012), multithreaded validation (2013), libsecp256k1 (8x faster crypto)
• Headers-first sync (2014) enabled downloading blocks from multiple peers simultaneously
• assumevalid (2017) cut IBD time roughly in half by making signature verification optional for older blocks
• Custom memory allocator (2022) delivered 21% faster IBD with better cache efficiency
• Performance bottlenecks shift over time - from CPU-bound signature verification to IO-bound chainstate access
• Bitcoin Core uses extensive benchmarking across different hardware configurations to validate optimizations

items.title: Sophisticated iPhone Hacking Toolkit Linked to US Government Infects Tens of Thousands
items.summary: A highly advanced iPhone exploitation toolkit, likely developed for the US government, has reportedly infected tens of thousands of devices in a mass surveillance operation.
items.content_text:
Sophisticated iPhone hacking techniques discovered affecting tens of thousands of phones
• Evidence suggests toolkit was originally built for US government use
• Scale indicates potential mass surveillance operation rather than targeted attacks
• Advanced exploitation capabilities demonstrate state-level technical resources
• Represents significant privacy threat to iPhone users globally
• Highlights ongoing government investment in mobile device compromise tools

items.title: Far-Right Think Tank Helped Craft DOJ Antifa Terror Indictment Against ICE Protesters
items.summary: Researcher from Islamophobic think tank provided language for first-ever domestic terrorism case against alleged antifa cell, citing Signal use and security practices as evidence.
items.content_text:
Kyle Shideler from Center for Security Policy helped DOJ craft terror indictment against Texas ICE protesters
• First domestic terrorism case against purported antifa cell involves 9 defendants facing life sentences
• Charges stem from July 4 protest at Prairieland ICE detention center where officer was shot
• Shideler testified Signal messaging, black bloc clothing, and security culture are 'antifa hallmarks'
• Center for Security Policy founded by Frank Gaffney, labeled hate group by SPLC for Islamophobic views
• Defense highlighted Shideler lacks academic credentials, doesn't use peer review or social science methods
• Prosecutors using privacy tools and operational security as evidence of extremist activity
• Case demonstrates concerning cooperation between federal prosecutors and far-right advocacy groups

items.title: EFF Executive Director Releases Privacy Rights Memoir 'Privacy's Defender'
items.summary: EFF's Cindy Cohn is launching a book chronicling her 30-year battle for digital privacy rights, covering crypto wars, surveillance state resistance, and EFF's evolution into a major digital rights organization.
items.content_text:
Cindy Cohn, EFF Executive Director, releasing 'Privacy's Defender' memoir in March 2026
• Book covers 30-year fight for digital privacy rights and chronicles crypto wars history
• Details EFF's growth from 'ragtag group of lawyers and hackers' to major digital rights org
• Cohn led successful First Amendment challenge to crypto export restrictions in Bernstein v. DOJ case
• Memoir addresses post-9/11 surveillance state and FBI gag orders
• Central question: 'Can we still have private conversations if we live our lives online?'
• Book launch event March 12, 2026 in Berkeley with conversation between Cohn and Annalee Newitz
• Proceeds benefit EFF's digital rights mission

items.title: Lugano Expands Bitcoin Infrastructure with $6.3M Plan ₿ Phase II
items.summary: Tether and Lugano launch Phase II expansion targeting digital sovereignty through decentralized infrastructure, privacy-preserving identity, and AI agents by 2030.
items.content_text:
Tether commits CHF 5M ($6.3M) for Lugano's Plan ₿ Phase II (2026-2030) digital infrastructure expansion
• Over 400 Lugano merchants now accept Bitcoin, USDT, and LVGA token after 4-year pilot program
• Five strategic pillars: institutional blockchain (SwissLedger), digital trade, privacy-preserving identity, decentralized AI, resilient urban infrastructure
• Privacy pillar uses zero-knowledge technologies for voluntary citizen/business verification
• PoW.space hub has attracted 100+ blockchain companies to the city
• Mayor explicitly frames initiative as preserving 'freedom' through data sovereignty and service autonomy
• Demonstrates municipal-scale adoption of freedom tech infrastructure in European context

items.title: Gov't Uses Privacy Tools as Evidence in First Domestic Terror Case Against Antifa Cell
items.summary: Federal prosecutors worked with far-right think tank researcher to craft terror charges against Texas activists, citing use of Signal and security practices as evidence of antifa membership.
items.content_text:
First-ever domestic terrorism case against alleged 'antifa cell' involves nine defendants facing life sentences for ICE protest
• Kyle Shideler from Center for Security Policy helped DOJ prosecutors write terror indictment language
• Government expert witness cited Signal use, 'black bloc' clothing, and 'security culture' as evidence of antifa practices
• Center for Security Policy is classified as hate group by SPLC, founded by 'Islamophobic conspiracy theorist' Frank Gaffney
• Defendants used fireworks at solidarity demonstration outside Prairieland ICE detention center; officer was shot
• Shideler acknowledged he doesn't use academic methods, peer review, or verify open-source materials authenticity
• Several cooperating defendants testified they didn't consider themselves antifa members
• Case demonstrates how privacy tools and operational security practices are being criminalized as terrorism evidence

items.title: Zimbabwe Escalates Crackdown on Opposition to Presidential Term Extension
items.summary: Zimbabwean authorities are using violence, intimidation, and surveillance against civil society groups opposing a constitutional amendment that would extend President Mnangagwa's rule until 2030.
items.content_text:
Zimbabwe gazetted Constitutional Amendment No 3 on February 16, 2026, extending presidential/parliamentary terms from 5 to 7 years
• Armed men in balaclavas raided National Constitutional Assembly offices, beating leader Lovemore Madhuku who filed court challenge
• Police shut down Constitutional Defenders Forum meetings using Maintenance of Peace and Order Act requirements
• Armed police besieged opposition lawyer Tendai Biti's offices, with reports of assaults and death threats
• SAPES Trust offices suffered suspected arson attack hours before hosting civil society dialogue on constitutional crisis
• Police require 5-7 days advance notice for meetings, selectively enforced to prevent opposition organizing
• Pattern demonstrates state using violence and legal tools to suppress democratic opposition to power consolidation
• Creates conditions where secure communications and coordination tools become essential for civil society survival

items.title: FBI Uses Film Review as 'Terror Evidence' Against Anti-ICE Protesters
items.summary: FBI seized a horror movie review from protester's home and prosecutors used it as evidence of 'ideologically driven intent' in terrorism case against alleged 'antifa cell.'
items.content_text:
FBI raided home of Daniel Sanchez Estrada, anti-ICE protester facing up to 20 years for 'transporting Antifa materials'
• Prosecutors introduced feminist film review about horror movies as evidence of terrorism in federal trial
• Review titled 'The Satanic Death-Cult Is Real' was seized alongside computers and weapons in itemized evidence list
• Case stems from July 4 protest at ICE's Prairieland Detention Facility where police officer was shot
• 18 people arrested in what prosecutors call first terrorism charges against alleged 'antifa cell'
• Estrada not accused of shooting but charged with conspiracy to conceal zines - First Amendment protected materials
• Trump administration designated 'antifa' as terrorist organization despite it being orientation, not formal group
• Pattern shows government using 'guilt by literature' tactics to criminalize possession of political writings

items.title: Iranian Women Footballers Granted Asylum After Protest, Facing State Surveillance
items.summary: Australia granted asylum to 5 Iranian women's football players and 1 official who protested by refusing to sing national anthem, amid reports of Iranian security following them and ongoing internet shutdowns in Iran.
items.content_text:
Five Iranian women's football players and one official granted asylum by Australia after seeking protection following March 8 match
• Players protested by refusing to sing national anthem during March 2 match against South Korea
• Iran's state television labeled the squad 'wartime traitors' for their silent protest
• Players reported being followed by Iranian security and handlers since arriving in Australia
• Protest echoed Iranian men's team actions at 2022 Qatar World Cup during 'Woman, Life, Freedom' uprising
• Timeline coincides with conflict in Iran starting February 28 and ongoing internet shutdowns
• Human Rights Watch confirms Iranian government deploys political 'minders' to monitor national team athletes
• FIFPRO criticizes FIFA and AFC for inadequate athlete protection protocols

items.title: US Treasury Acknowledges Legitimate Uses of Crypto Mixers in Congressional Report
items.summary: U.S. Treasury told Congress that crypto mixers serve legitimate privacy purposes, marking a significant policy shift while proposing new 'hold law' for suspicious digital assets.
items.content_text:
Treasury submitted 32-page report to Congress acknowledging legitimate privacy uses for crypto mixers
• Major shift from previous stance that led to Tornado Cash sanctions in 2022
• Report cites lawful uses including protecting personal wealth info and business payment privacy
• Proposes 'hold law' giving financial institutions safe harbor to freeze suspicious digital assets
• Treasury data shows $37.4B in bridge withdrawals since 2020, with $1.6B from mixing services
• DPRK-linked actors stole $2.8B in digital assets Jan 2024-Sep 2025, heavily using mixers
• No new restrictions recommended for non-custodial mixers; FinCEN 2023 recordkeeping rule not finalized
• Comes after Tornado Cash sanctions lifted in March 2025 following federal appeals court ruling

items.title: Iran's 98% Internet Shutdown Drives Push for Direct-to-Cell Satellite Tech
items.summary: Iran imposed a near-total internet blackout during conflict, prompting digital rights advocates to urgently call for Direct-to-Cell satellite technology that connects smartphones directly to satellites, bypassing government shutdowns.
items.content_text:
Iran cut internet connectivity by 98% starting February 28, 2026 following U.S.-Israel military attacks
• Access Now condemns the shutdown affecting millions during critical conflict period
• Blackout blocks journalists and human rights defenders from documenting violations
• Information vacuum enables disinformation spread and hampers accountability efforts
• Access Now advocates for Direct-to-Cell (D2C) satellite technology as solution
• D2C connects ordinary smartphones directly to satellites, bypassing censorship
• Civil society coalition calls for accelerated humanitarian deployment of D2C tech
• Pattern continues Iran's history of using shutdowns to control crisis information

items.title: EFF Analysis: SAFE Act Proposes Major Section 702 Surveillance Reforms
items.summary: The bipartisan SAFE Act would require FBI warrants for accessing Americans' communications collected via Section 702, address parallel construction, and partially close data broker loopholes.
items.content_text:
Senators Lee (R-UT) and Durbin (D-IL) introduced SAFE Act to reform Section 702 FISA surveillance authority
• Bill would require FBI warrants before accessing content of Americans' incidentally collected communications
• Would mandate disclosure of Section 702 evidence in court proceedings to prevent parallel construction
• Includes provisions to close data broker loophole for intelligence agencies purchasing location/personal data
• Section 702 expires April 2026, creating window for reform vs White House push for clean reauthorization
• FBI conducted 3.4 million warrantless searches of US persons' 702 data in 2021 alone
• EFF calls reforms imperfect but significant improvement over unconstitutional status quo
• Federal court recently ruled backdoor searches of 702 data unconstitutional

items.title: Azerbaijani Journalist Under Armed Surveillance in Switzerland
items.summary: Exiled Azerbaijani journalist Emin Huseynov reports being followed by armed men in Switzerland, highlighting transnational repression risks for freedom tech builders and activists.
items.content_text:
Emin Huseynov, co-founder of Institute for Reporters' Freedom and Safety, reports surveillance by armed men in Geneva
• Huseynov filmed one incident, posted footage to YouTube as evidence
• Swiss police investigating multiple surveillance episodes reported by Huseynov
• His brother Mehman Huseynov publishing critical videos of Azerbaijani president, living under police protection
• Pattern follows previous Azerbaijani state-linked assassination attempts against exiled journalist Mahammad Mirzali
• 2017 precedent: journalist Afgan Mukhtarli abducted from Georgia, linked to Azerbaijani authorities
• Demonstrates operational security failures and need for better protection protocols for exiled activists
• Azerbaijan ranks as world's 6th worst jailer of journalists with 24 currently imprisoned

items.title: Freedom House Documents Artists Imprisoned for Creative Dissent Across 7 Authoritarian States
items.summary: Freedom House catalogs political imprisonment of artists in Russia, Belarus, Cuba, Azerbaijan, Egypt, China, and Venezuela, highlighting systematic state repression of creative expression that challenges power.
items.content_text:
Freedom House documents crackdowns on artists across 7 countries: Russia, Belarus, Cuba, Azerbaijan, Egypt, China, and Venezuela
• Luis Manuel Otero Alcántara, Cuban artist and San Isidro Movement cofounder, imprisoned since July 11, 2021 protests
• Sentenced to 5 years for 'contempt, public disorder, and insulting symbols of the homeland' after protesting Decree 348
• Maykel Osorbo Castillo Pérez, musician and co-writer of protest song 'Patria y Vida', arrested 121 times in 5 months
• Cuba's Decree 348 (2018) requires government pre-approval for even private artistic performances
• Artists targeted because creative work can 'expose, ridicule, and condemn in emotive and powerful ways'
• Pattern shows systematic use of state machinery to silence criticism through imprisonment of creative dissidents

items.title: OpenAI Pentagon Deal Raises Mass Surveillance Concerns Despite CEO Denials
items.summary: OpenAI's new Pentagon contract permits bulk data collection from users, with experts warning about AI-amplified surveillance risks despite CEO Sam Altman's claims otherwise.
items.content_text:
OpenAI signed Pentagon contract allowing bulk user data collection [Citizen Lab]
• CEO Sam Altman denies deal enables mass surveillance capabilities
• Senior researcher Wolfie Christl and other experts express skepticism about Altman's claims
• Government can already purchase personal data for warrantless surveillance operations
• AI systems amplify surveillance risks through 'errors, bias, and lack of accountability'
• Represents potential policy shift in OpenAI's previous surveillance red lines
• Opaque AI processing makes harm from surveillance data more difficult to detect

items.title: Anonymous Credentials: Privacy-Preserving Auth Against Age Verification Laws
items.summary: Cryptographer Matthew Green explains anonymous credentials as essential defense against privacy dystopia created by age verification laws requiring government ID for most online activities.
items.content_text:
25 U.S. states and dozen+ countries now require age verification using government ID for 'inappropriate' content
• Laws originally targeting porn now affect social media, encyclopedias (Wikipedia facing UK Online Safety Bill)
• Traditional authentication creates surveillance trail linking real identity to all online activity
• Anonymous credentials allow proving authorization (age, humanity) without revealing specific identity
• Based on 1980s work by David Chaum, breaks linkage between credential issuance and usage
• User shows ID once to issuer, receives unlinkable credential for repeated anonymous access
• Provides anonymity set protection - site knows user has valid credential but not which specific user
• Critical for preventing advertising-driven retention of identity data despite legal data minimization

items.title: EFF Warns Against Meta Smart Glasses Over Privacy and Surveillance Concerns
items.summary: EFF raises alarm about Meta's Ray-Ban smart glasses, highlighting that footage is reviewed by human contractors, used for AI training, and enables covert surveillance of bystanders.
items.content_text:
Meta's Ray-Ban smart glasses automatically upload footage to Meta AI app by default
• Human contractors review and annotate sensitive footage including nudity and bathroom visits for AI training
• Glasses designed to look like regular eyewear, making recording nearly invisible to bystanders
• Small recording indicator light can be disabled with cheap hardware hacks
• Audio conversations with Meta AI are saved by default and must be manually deleted
• Footage may also reach Apple/Google servers via phone storage, potentially accessible to law enforcement
• Google partnering with Warby Parker and Apple reportedly developing competing products
• EFF warns constant recording creates new privacy risks beyond traditional phone cameras
